summary: Patrick McKenzie presents an advanced SEO framework for software companies, arguing that the core equation is simple: Content + Links = Success. He advocates treating company websites as first-class software products deserving continuous improvement, and demonstrates how scalable content generation through CMS systems, outsourced writing, and automation can turn SEO into a predictable revenue channel. Using his own Bingo Card Creator as a case study, he shows how paying a freelancer $3,000 over several years generated over $20,000 in sales in one year alone. He also covers linkbait strategies, particularly open source software as a uniquely effective link-building tool for developers.
key_points: SEO fundamentals reduce to a simple equation: Content + Links = You Win, but benefits compound multiplicatively rather than linearly
The biggest SEO mistake software companies make is treating their website as a second-class citizen — whipped up at 2 AM on release day and never touched again
Scalable content generation through outsourcing and automation turns SEO into a channel like PPC: invest money, measure returns, reinvest profits — except you keep SEO gains forever
The best content is modular, evergreen, competitively defensible, and process-oriented — treating content as durable capital rather than disposable news

Open source software is uniquely powerful linkbait because it's hard to duplicate, consultants will cite your site to justify their billing rates, and first movers in new categories capture disproportionate mindshare
money_quotes: People treat SEO like it is black magic, but at the core it is very simple: Content + Links = You Win.
The biggest single problem with software company's SEO is that they treat their website like a second class citizen.
You'd never do calculations by counting on your fingers when you have a computer available to do them for you. Hand-writing all content in Notepad is essentially the same.
The difference is that you get to keep your SEO gains forever rather than having to rebuy them on every click like PPC.
You are expensive. English majors are cheap.
OSS which solves problems for businesses tends to pick up links from, e.g., consultants deploying it — they will cite your website to justify their billing rate.
The first semi-decent OSS in a particular category tends to win a lot of the links and mindshare. So get cracking!
key_insight: Software companies should treat SEO as a scalable, automatable revenue channel by combining systematic content generation with strategic link-building, turning small investments in outsourced content into compounding long-term returns.
